title: A Symmetric Unified Transport and Charge Model for MOSFETs from Diffusive to Ballistic Regimes
abstract: This paper presents a symmetric unified transport (UT) compact model for MOSFETs that bridges drift-diffusion (DD) and ballistic transport (BT) regimes. The proposed model self consistently accounts for both current and charge across the DD-BT transition. Quantum capacitance and carrier transport are incorporated into the charge density formulation. Drain side velocity saturation and the source side thermal velocity limit are unified within a single framework using a physically motivated high field scattering length, enabling accurate modeling from DD square law behavior to the ballistic limit. In addition, a physical channel charge and capacitance model is developed to capture capacitance reduction in the quasi ballistic regime, which is not considered in standard compact models. The model is verified using theoretical analysis and experimental data from MOSFETs with multiple channel lengths, achieving accurate fitting using only physical transport parameters. The formulation is continuous and symmetric, and it passes both DC and AC symmetry tests.
summary: This paper proposes a new mathematical framework for modeling MOSFETs that works across all channel lengths, from large-scale transistors to extremely small, ballistic ones. It solves a common problem in semiconductor modeling where models become discontinuous or physically inaccurate at very small scales. By unifying drift-diffusion and ballistic transport through a symmetric approach, the model accurately predicts current and capacitance. The model is validated using experimental data from advanced technologies like FinFETs.
main_problem: The need for a unified compact model for MOSFETs that smoothly and physically describes carrier transport across the transition from the diffusive (long-channel) to the ballistic (short-channel) regime while maintaining DC and AC symmetry.
main_result: The proposed Symmetric Unified Transport (UT) model successfully bridges the drift-diffusion and ballistic regimes, capturing velocity overshoot and capacitance reduction while passing both DC and AC symmetry tests.
method: The model uses Matthiessen's rule to combine drift-diffusion and ballistic velocities and employs a weighted sum approach to connect charge distributions, incorporating quantum capacitance and a high-field scattering length.
model_or_system: The physical system is MOSFETs (including FinFET, FDSOI, and GAA architectures) with channel lengths ranging from micrometer to sub-20-nm scales.
key_observables: Drain-source current (Ids), gate capacitance (Cgg), threshold voltage (Vt), effective mobility, and charge distributions.
important_parameters: Transition from Drift-Diffusion (DD) to Ballistic Transport (BT) regimes; thermal injection velocity (vth); high-field scattering length (lambda_sat); channel lengths from 1 nm to 2 um.
main_assumptions: Uses 1D Poisson and charge sheet approximations; assumes the scattering length L can be used in the transmission rate instead of a voltage-dependent critical length; GCA-based charge density is not applicable under fully ballistic conditions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows model verification; Figure 8 compares model predictions to experimental data for Intel 3 FinFET Id-Vds and Id-Vgs; Figure 9 displays DC and AC symmetry test results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem of transport modeling, presents the three-part unified transport and charge model, details the mathematical formulation for velocity and charge, validates the model against theoretical limits and experimental data from various MOSFET technologies, and concludes with symmetry test results.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 verifies the model behavior in the long-channel limit by comparing the IdVd and CggiVg (intrinsic gate capacitance) curves with those of the DD model. At L=2 m, and the device operates in the fully diffusive regime. In this case, (7a) reduces to 𝑣dd, and the channel charge fully relaxes to local equilibrium. Consequently, the UT model reproduces the classic square-law behavior, yielding the same IV and CV curves as the DD model.
